turns.content: I'm trying to build a job scheduler that processes thousands of log files, but I'm running into performance bottlenecks. My Java app uses a standard HashMap for caching results, and it gets overwhelmed quickly when the cache size exceeds memory limits.

That's a classic problem with pure in-memory caches under high load. You're right that HashMaps can't handle that scale without spilling to disk or losing data. Since you're exploring Rust, the crate foyer has a hybrid cache implementation that combines an LRU in-memory section with a tiered disk storage layer.
I see, so I can just replace my HashMap with a foyer instance and it will auto-manage the overflow? That sounds too easy for what I'm dealing with. Can you explain how the eviction policy works?

It's not that simple, but the design philosophy is sound. The foyer crate uses a configurable cache admission policy where entries are evicted from memory only when they exceed a threshold and can be safely written to disk. You need to implement the Cache trait yourself to handle the actual read/write operations between layers.
Wait, I thought the whole point of crates like foyer was that you just drop into them? Why am I still writing all this boilerplate for the eviction logic?

You're right to feel frustrated. The crate provides the core data structures and async-safe abstractions, but it doesn't magically implement your business logic. You must provide the actual storage backend implementation that satisfies the Cache trait requirements for both memory and disk layers.
Okay, I understand now. So I need to write my own storage handler for the hybrid cache? And this async compatibility thing is critical because my scheduler runs on async runtime?

Exactly. The foyer crate guarantees thread safety and async compatibility through careful interior mutability patterns, but you still need to implement your specific storage backends. For the disk layer, you could use something like tokio::fs or a database client that implements the required trait bounds.
This is getting complicated. I was hoping for some magic Rust abstractions to save me time on this repetitive task automation. Is there any way to simplify this?

The complexity comes from the fact that you're building a real system, not just using an API. However, once you've written your storage handlers, foyer does handle most of the heavy lifting around concurrency and memory management. You might consider starting with a simpler in-memory cache first to validate your logic before adding disk persistence.
I think I get it now. So my path forward is to implement both storage layers for foyer, then integrate it into my scheduler? But I'm worried about the async integration complexity.

You're right to be cautious. The async part of foyer uses tokio's runtime patterns which are well-documented in their examples. Start with a minimal working example using just the memory layer, then gradually add your disk storage implementation and verify that all operations remain non-blocking.

Hey, I'm building a proof-of-concept for our startup and currently using a simple in-memory cache wrapped around the Json serialization with serde_json::Value. It works fine until we hit high load, so I've been looking into foyer to add disk persistence and see if it can handle concurrent access better without blocking threads.

That makes sense because standard in-memory caches like Arc<Mutex<HashMap<T>>> have contention issues under high concurrency. Foyer is designed specifically to solve this by combining an LRU cache with a disk-backed store, so you get fast access for hot items while older ones are flushed to the filesystem asynchronously without blocking your async runtime.

I see, but I'm confused about how the admission policy works. If we use foyer::hybrid_cache with a default LRU eviction, will that actually keep our hot data in memory or just write everything to disk eventually? How do we control which items stay in RAM?

The admission policy is the key because it determines exactly how many entries are allowed in the LRU section before eviction happens. You can configure this by setting a limit on the number of keys, so only your most frequently accessed items like user sessions or API tokens stay in memory while the rest are moved to disk storage.
Got it, but what if we need more flexibility? Can we use a custom cache admission policy instead of the default LRU so that items with higher read rates are preferred over write-heavy ones?

Yes, you can absolutely implement your own trait for the cache admission policy by defining a struct that implements CacheAdmissionPolicy. This allows you to weigh different metrics like access frequency and data size when deciding whether to keep an item in memory or evict it based on custom business rules.

That sounds promising, but I'm worried about performance overhead from the disk sync. Since we're using async runtime with tokio::spawn, will the background flushing of the cache to disk cause any blocking or latency spikes in our main thread?

Foyer is designed to be fully async-compatible because it uses non-blocking I/O and futures for its internal operations. The disk flush happens asynchronously via tokio::spawn channels, so your main application threads never block waiting for the cache to persist; they just see immediate results while the background thread handles the heavy lifting.

Okay, that clears up the async concern. But I'm still wondering about error handling—if a disk write fails or the filesystem is full, does foyer automatically retry or should we catch those errors and handle them explicitly in our application logic?

Foyer doesn't silently ignore failures; instead, it returns an Option<T> where T is your cached value. If the disk write fails or the filesystem is full, the cache entry will simply not be returned until a successful flush occurs later, so you need to handle these cases explicitly in your application logic by checking if the result contains None.
I understand now. One last thing—since we're comparing this against my current serde_json::Value based solution, will migrating to foyer significantly reduce memory usage or just improve latency?

It should do both because the hybrid architecture means only recently accessed items consume RAM while older data sits on disk. For your startup's use case where you expect occasional stale data, this will likely reduce peak memory usage by 30-50% compared to a pure in-memory cache while also improving latency for frequently changing hot paths.
I'm trying to initialize a hybrid cache using the foyer crate but I keep getting an error saying that the underlying storage isn't compatible with async operations. What went wrong?

The issue is likely that you are trying to use a blocking storage backend directly inside an async context without proper handling. The foyer crate requires storage backends like FmtStorage or DiskStorage to be configured correctly for async compatibility, specifically by implementing the AsyncStorage trait. If your current setup uses a standard file system path without wrapping it in an asynchronous abstraction layer, the runtime will panic because it cannot yield control while waiting on I/O.
I thought since foyer supports async, I could just use any disk backend. I wrote code assuming that opening a file path would automatically make it async-compatible like the in-memory store.

That is a common misconception about how Rust handles concurrency and system calls. Think of it like trying to drive a car with automatic transmission by manually shifting gears at every stoplight; the engine will stall because the mechanism isn't designed for that flow. The in-memory store is like driving on a straight highway where you never have to shift, but disk storage requires a specific gear (the async abstraction) to function smoothly. You cannot simply treat a blocking file operation as if it were non-blocking without introducing race conditions or deadlocks.
So I need to replace my simple file open with something that implements the Async trait? But what exactly does that look like in code?

You don't write raw file operations; you use crates that wrap standard file system calls into async interfaces. For example, instead of using std::fs::File which blocks the thread, you would import a crate like tokio::fs or use the async-aware version provided by foyer's storage traits. The key is ensuring your storage backend implementation returns futures rather than completing synchronously when data arrives on disk.
Wait, so I can't just pass a path string to foyer? Does it have a specific constructor that expects an async-capable trait?

Yes, the foyer crate's HybridCache type is constructed by passing a storage backend that implements the required traits. You cannot instantiate the cache with a raw path string directly. Instead, you must create an instance of a concrete storage implementation like DiskStorage or FmtStorage which internally handles the conversion from your synchronous file system expectations to asynchronous futures.
This is getting complicated. I was expecting something simple where I just drop in the crate and forget about threading.

Rust's type system doesn't allow that level of abstraction by default because it needs to guarantee safety against data races and deadlocks. Think of it like building a house: you can't just say 'I want a roof' and expect the builder to magically know how to nail it without following specific rules about beam spacing and load distribution. Every component, including your storage backend, must adhere to strict interface contracts defined by traits.
Okay, so I need to understand the trait bounds first before writing the code. Is there a way to check what traits my current backend satisfies?

You can use compiler diagnostics and trait implementations to verify this. When you attempt to instantiate the HybridCache with your current storage type, if it fails to implement the necessary bounds like AsyncStorage or CacheStore, the compiler will explicitly list which traits are missing. This is how Rust helps you identify exactly what gaps exist in your implementation.
I'm frustrated because I spent hours debugging this and realized my assumption was wrong about how async works with files.

It is natural to feel that way when moving from high-level languages or simplified libraries to low-level systems programming. The frustration comes from the gap between intuitive expectations and strict type safety requirements. However, once you internalize the concept of async-aware abstractions, this complexity becomes a feature rather than a bug because it prevents subtle concurrency issues.
Can I just use an existing crate that provides these wrappers instead of writing my own storage layer?

Yes, there are mature crates like tokio's fs module or async-disk-cache implementations that provide ready-to-use wrappers around standard file operations. These crates implement the necessary trait bounds so you can plug them into foyer without having to manually handle low-level synchronization primitives yourself.
So I should probably start with one of those wrapper crates and only write custom logic for the cache admission policy?

Exactly. Use a proven async file wrapper to handle the raw storage layer, then focus your mental energy on designing the cache admission policy logic which determines when entries should be evicted or kept based on memory pressure and disk usage thresholds.
I still don't fully grasp why async is strictly required for disk storage here. Why can't we just run the cache in a separate thread?

Because Rust's runtime model discourages manual threading for performance and complexity reasons. The standard library provides an async runtime that handles all the scheduling and yielding automatically. By using async storage, you leverage this built-in mechanism to avoid manually managing thread pools or locks, which reduces overhead and makes the code easier to reason about.
Okay, I think I understand now. Let me try setting up an async-aware disk storage backend with foyer again.

That sounds like a good plan. Once you successfully instantiate the HybridCache with a proper async-backed storage implementation, you should be able to proceed with configuring the cache admission policy without hitting those initial errors again.
I'm trying to understand the difference between foyer and a standard in-memory cache like Arc<Mutex<T>> or a simple HashMap.

The main distinction is that foyer implements a hybrid cache strategy combining in-memory storage with persistent disk backing, whereas a basic Arc or HashMap exists only in RAM. While you can wrap data in an Arc<Mutex<T>> to share ownership and use Mutex<T> for thread safety within a single process, those don't provide the persistence or async-safe concurrent access patterns that foyer offers out of the box.
So if I want to persist data across restarts without writing my own file IO logic, does foyer handle that automatically?

Yes, foyer manages the persistence layer for you using a configuration struct that defines where your cache files live on disk. You don't need to implement custom serialization or file writing; it handles the async-safe storage and retrieval internally.
That sounds convenient, but I've heard people struggle with cache admission policies in hybrid systems. How does foyer handle that?

Foyer provides built-in support for various cache admission policies like LRU or LFU through its configuration options. When the disk reaches capacity, it can automatically evict items based on these policies before blocking new writes.
Wait, I thought eviction was something you had to manage manually with a custom trait or impl Cache? Isn't that where beginners make mistakes?

That's actually a common misconception. Beginners often think they need to write their own cache traits or implement custom evict logic using traits like Send or Sync manually, but foyer abstracts this away so you just configure the policy without touching the low-level implementation details.
Can I still customize which items get evicted if my application has specific business rules?

Yes, foyer allows you to extend its behavior by implementing custom eviction strategies that respect your domain logic. You can create specialized cache types or use the extensible configuration system to define how items are removed when memory pressure occurs.
Does this work well in async environments like tokio without blocking threads?

Absolutely, foyer is designed to be fully async-compatible. It integrates seamlessly with runtime systems like tokio so your cache operations don't block event loops or require thread pools for disk I/O.
One last question: If I use foyer, do I still need to worry about serialization formats like bincode or serde_json when saving to disk?

You still need to serialize your data types in a way that foyer can read them back from the cache files, but you don't have to implement any special traits for the storage layer itself. Just ensure your values implement standard serialization traits and they'll persist correctly.

I've been porting my old Python caching logic to Rust using the foyer crate. I managed to implement a hybrid cache that stores items in memory and flushes them to disk when full, but I'm wondering if there are better patterns for handling this with async.

That's great progress! Since you're using foyer, you have access to the AsyncCache trait which makes things much cleaner. Instead of manually managing disk writes in a blocking way, you can leverage the async-aware storage backends that foyer supports.
Right, so I'm currently using std::fs::File for persistence and then checking LRU metrics every loop iteration to decide whether to evict. That feels clunky though.
Exactly, that's not idiomatic Rust. A better approach is to use the CacheAdmissionPolicy trait provided by foyer for managing space constraints automatically rather than polling manually.
I've read about CacheAdmissionPolicy but haven't tried it yet. How does that actually work in practice?
Think of it like a bouncer at a club who decides exactly when someone gets kicked out based on the current crowd size, instead of you manually counting heads every minute.
That analogy makes sense. So I should configure this policy and let foyer handle the eviction logic?
Precisely. You set up a CacheAdmissionPolicy that defines your admission rules, then pass it into the AsyncCache setup so it manages space dynamically without blocking threads.
What about memory management? Do I still need to manually call drop() or something?
No, just like with any async object in Rust, you let the runtime handle cleanup through Arc and Send traits. The AsyncCache wrapper ensures proper synchronization between threads.
Wait, I'm confused about how the hybrid part works. Does it automatically know when to write to disk?

Yes, that's the beauty of foyer's design. Once you bind your storage backend with the AsyncCache trait and set an LRU policy, it handles both in-memory access and periodic flushing to persistent storage seamlessly.
So I don't need custom code for disk persistence at all?
Not really, you just configure the backend and the cache admission policy. The crate handles the heavy lifting of managing the hybrid state correctly.
That's surprisingly elegant compared to what I had before. Is there anything else about async safety I should worry about?
Just ensure your storage backend implements the AsyncStorage trait properly so that all read/write operations are non-blocking and thread-safe.
Got it. So my final architecture would be: AsyncCache + foyer's LRU policy + a custom async storage backend?
Exactly, that combination gives you the best of both worlds - automatic space management with proper async safety guarantees.

I'm trying to build a tool that caches API responses but I keep hitting disk latency issues. My current approach uses the standard library's HashMap which is fast in memory but crashes if the process dies, and I can't afford that for this high-throughput automation task.

It sounds like you need a solution that persists to disk automatically while keeping the primary data structure in memory. The standard library's HashMap doesn't have persistence, so your process would lose everything on crash. For this kind of hybrid approach where you want async compatibility and automatic spill-over, the foyer crate is designed specifically for this pattern.

I've looked at foyer but I'm confused about how it handles the transition between memory and disk. Is there a specific trait I need to implement to make my existing code work with it without rewriting everything?

You don't actually need to rewrite your core logic if you are using a hybrid cache strategy correctly. The key is that foyer provides a trait called Cache for the async-compatible interface, and when combined with DiskCache it handles the spill-over automatically. You can just implement Serialize on your data structures since foyer relies heavily on serde serialization internally.

That helps but I'm still worried about memory management. What happens if my cache grows too large? Does it actually spill to disk seamlessly or do I have to manually track which items are in RAM versus on the SSD?

The whole point of foyer's hybrid model is that you don't manually manage the split. When your in-memory cache exceeds a configured threshold, it automatically spills excess entries to the disk layer. You only need to track how much memory you are using and set limits on the memory pool.
So I just need to configure the memory limit and let foyer handle the rest? Can I still use standard Rust collections like Vec or HashMap for my cache values?

Yes, that is exactly how you do it. You can keep your internal data structures as regular Rust types like Vec or HashMap, but when you wrap them in a Cache from the foyer crate with DiskCache configured, they automatically get backed by both memory and disk.
I'm still not sure about the admission policy though. How does foyer decide which items to evict if I have multiple keys? Is it LRU or something else?

By default, foyer uses an LRU-like eviction strategy for the in-memory portion when combined with DiskCache. However, you can also configure custom admission policies if your use case requires more complex rules like LFU or time-based expiration.
I'm frustrated because I thought I could just drop the standard library and move everything to foyer but it seems too much work. Is there a way to keep my existing logic mostly intact?

You are correct that you don't need to rewrite your entire application. The best approach is to identify the specific data structures that hold the cache values and wrap only those in foyer's Cache type while keeping the rest of your logic using standard Rust types.

Okay so I keep my Vec for request tracking but replace the HashMap with a Cache<_, DiskCache>? But what about concurrency? My tool processes multiple requests at once and I'm worried about data races between memory and disk access.

That is a valid concern since you are mixing async operations. The key thing to remember is that foyer's Cache type is designed for async contexts, so it uses interior mutability patterns like RefCell internally to prevent data races while still allowing safe concurrent access from multiple threads or async tasks.
I'm still confused about the error handling though. If a disk write fails, what happens? Does my program just stop?

No, it does not just stop. The Cache type will catch those failures and log them while continuing to operate with whatever data is currently in memory. You can configure this behavior using the CacheResult enum which tells you whether an operation succeeded or failed.
Wait so I don't need to implement custom error handling logic? Just wrap my existing code?

You are right, you mainly just need to replace the standard library HashMap with a Cache instance. However, you do need to ensure your data structures implement Serialize for them to be usable with foyer's serialization layer.
I think I understand now but can you show me how to actually instantiate this in my code? Like what arguments does the Cache constructor take exactly?

For a basic hybrid cache setup, you would call Cache::new with your data type and then combine it with DiskCache using the appropriate builder pattern. The exact arguments depend on whether you want to use async or sync APIs but generally you pass in your data structure and configuration options.
I'm still not sure if I need to manually configure eviction thresholds or if there's a sensible default. Can you help me understand what parameters I should set for production use?

For most production scenarios, the defaults work well but you should definitely check your memory usage and adjust the memory limit based on your system constraints. You might want to increase the disk threshold if your SSD is fast enough to handle more writes without latency spikes.
Hey everyone, I'm new to Rust and just started onboarding to this codebase. I've read about the foyer crate but still don't fully understand how it works as a hybrid cache.

Welcome to the team! The foyer crate is indeed a powerful tool for building in-memory and disk hybrid caches. It allows you to manage hot data in RAM while persisting cold data to disk, which helps with performance and storage efficiency.
I think I understand now that it just uses a regular file system cache. So if I write to the cache, does it automatically go to disk like Linux's page cache?

Not exactly. While foyer can use the OS page cache for storage, its primary advantage is that you have full control over what data lives in memory versus disk through an admission policy. You can choose which items stay hot and which get evicted to disk based on custom rules.
Ah okay so I need to implement a cache admission policy myself? That sounds complicated compared to just letting the OS handle it.

Exactly. You define how items are admitted into memory and when they should be flushed to disk or removed entirely. For example, you could use a Least Recently Used (LRU) policy or a custom LRU-based eviction strategy tailored to your application needs.
Can I just write some code and forget about it? Or do I need to manage all the low-level disk operations manually?

You don't need to handle low-level details yourself. The foyer crate abstracts away most of that complexity, letting you focus on designing your admission policy at a higher level while still ensuring async compatibility.
Oh wait, I didn't realize it supports async operations too. Does that mean I can use it with Tokio or something similar?
Yes, absolutely. The foyer crate is designed to be async-compatible, meaning you can integrate it seamlessly into asynchronous Rust applications using crates like tokio.
That's great! But what if I need to check the cache status or get stats? Is there a way to do that without blocking?

You can use the async-safe methods provided by foyer to query cache metrics like hit rates, memory usage, and disk space consumption. These operations are non-blocking so they won't interfere with your async event loop.
I see. And if I want to clear the entire cache or reset it? Can that be done asynchronously too?
Yes, clearing the cache is also an async operation in foyer. You can invoke flush-to-disk or full-clear operations without blocking your main thread.
One more thing though—if I have a lot of data, will it automatically spill over to disk when memory runs out?
That depends on your configured admission policy. If you set up a capacity limit and an eviction strategy like LRU, then yes, older items can be written to disk or removed from the cache as needed.
Got it. So basically I just need to configure the policy and let foyer handle the rest. Pretty straightforward once you know how.
Exactly! Once you grasp the basics of defining your admission policy, using foyer becomes much simpler than managing raw file system caching yourself.
